skill.name: git-commit
skill.description: Create Conventional Commit messages by inspecting git diffs, staging selected files, and running git commit commands.
skill.summary: Generate and execute Conventional Commit messages from repository changes.
skill.author: github

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command and sensitive-data patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples for a git commit workflow. The confirmed risk is legitimate Bash-driven git operations that can stage files and create commits, so publication is acceptable with a repository-modification warning.

medium_findings.title: Bash Git Commands Modify Repository State
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run git diff, git status, git add, and git commit. These are expected for this skill, but they can stage files and create commits in the user repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly shown in the workflow and are core to the skill purpose. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the commands are standard git commands and no exfiltration path is present.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Mentions Are Protective Guidance
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged .env, credentials.json, and private keys. The text tells the agent never to commit these files, so this is a false positive rather than evidence of secret collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 83 is an explicit warning against committing secrets. I found no evidence of reading, storing, or transmitting secret values.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the referenced lines describe commit formatting, message length, or git workflow text. No cryptographic operation is defined.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line references contain natural-language git commit instructions and placeholders. I found no hashing, encryption, signing, or crypto library use.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution Through Bash
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is allowed to use Bash and provides git commands for inspecting diffs, staging files, and committing changes. This is required functionality, but users should review staged files before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Bash permission and git command examples are direct evidence of external command execution. The commands are scoped to git and are consistent with the declared purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution In Commit Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The multi-line commit example uses command substitution with a heredoc to pass a commit message. It appears to be documentation for a legitimate git command, not obfuscated execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell syntax is real, but the heredoc content is a commit message template. There is no evidence of hidden payloads or user-controlled command execution beyond the intended git commit.

content.value_statement: Writing clear commit messages and staging the right files can slow down development. This skill reviews diffs, suggests Conventional Commit messages, and helps create focused git commits.
actual_capabilities: Inspects staged and unstaged git diffs before creating a commit.
Suggests Conventional Commit type, scope, and description from changed files.
Stages selected files or interactive hunks when a commit needs logical grouping.
Creates single-line or multi-line git commits using the generated message.
Applies safety guidance for secrets, destructive commands, hooks, and force pushes.
limitations: Requires a local git repository with Bash access.
Can only commit changes that exist in the current workspace.
Does not guarantee that generated messages match every team convention.
Cannot prevent all accidental staging if the user approves broad file patterns.

use_cases.title: Prepare Feature Commits
use_cases.description: Group related feature changes and generate a clear Conventional Commit message.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Review local diffs and split unrelated edits into focused commits before review.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team History
use_cases.description: Use consistent commit types and scopes across routine repository work.

prompt_templates.title: Commit Current Changes
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a standard commit.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a git commit for the current changes. Review the diff, choose a Conventional Commit message, and show me what will be committed.

prompt_templates.title: Commit With Scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Commit these changes using the scope auth. Inspect the staged and unstaged diff, then generate the best Conventional Commit message.

prompt_templates.title: Split Logical Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my working tree and split the changes into separate logical commits. Stage only the files needed for the first commit.

prompt_templates.title: Create Release Commit
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced commit with body or footer.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Conventional Commit with a body and any needed issue references. Explain the selected type, scope, and staged files before committing.

output_examples.input: Commit the changes in this repository.
output_examples.output: The skill reviews git status and diff, stages the relevant files, and creates a commit such as "fix(auth): handle expired sessions".

output_examples.input: Make a docs commit for the README edits.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies documentation-only changes, stages the README updates, and commits them with a docs message.

output_examples.input: Split these edits into logical commits.
output_examples.output: The skill separates unrelated files, commits the first logical group, and leaves remaining changes available for the next commit.

best_practices: Review git status and staged files before approving a commit.
Keep each commit focused on one logical change.
Use explicit scopes when a repository has clear modules or packages.
anti_patterns: Do not commit secrets, credentials, private keys, or local environment files.
Do not stage broad file patterns without reviewing the matched files.
Do not bypass hooks or amend commits unless the user explicitly requests it.

faq.question: Does this skill create commits automatically?
faq.answer: It can run git commit after reviewing diffs and staging files. Users should review the planned commit before approval.

faq.question: Does it support Conventional Commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It selects a type, optional scope, short description, body, and footer when appropriate.

faq.question: Can it split unrelated changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It can stage selected files or hunks so each commit has one logical purpose.

faq.question: Will it commit secrets?
faq.answer: The skill explicitly warns against committing .env files, credential files, and private keys. Users should still review staged files.

faq.question: Does it push commits to a remote repository?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow creates local commits and warns against force pushing to protected branches.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
